texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aiming at a more thorough understanding of the influence of zeolite fillers in PDMS membranes on the pervaporation of chlorinated hydrocarbons from aqueous solutions, the sorption aspect of this separation process was studied. Firstly, the sorption of water and five different chlorinated hydrocarbons was investigated in the zeolite and the polymer separately. Secondly, the composite system was considered and conclusions were drawn concerning interactions at the zeolite -polymer interphase. Finally, the sorption results obtained on the self-synthesized membranes were compared with measurements on commercially available PDMS membranes (GFT).
texts.text: Aiming at a more thorough understanding of the influence of zeolite fillers in PDMS membranes on the pervaporation of chlorinated hydrocarbons from aqueous solutions, the sorption aspect of this separation process was studied. Firstly, the sorption of water and five different chlorinated hydrocarbons was investigated in the zeolite and the polymer separately. Secondly, the composite system was considered and conclusions were drawn concerning interactions at the zeolite -polymer interphase. Finally, the sorption results obtained on the self-synthesized membranes were compared with measurements on commercially available PDMS membranes (GFT).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Materials and Methods
texts.text: Materials and Methods

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Materials. 1. Zeolites. ZSM-5 and silicalite were obtained from PQ Corp. (coded as CBV-3020 and CBV-3002, respectively) and zeolite Y was obtained from Zeocat. Table 1 lists their main characteristics. Both PQ zeolites have the same structure, consisting of intersecting channels forming a threedimensional network. They differ in hydrophobic character since a higher degree of isomorphous substitution (Al for Si) in the zeolite framework of CBV-3020 results in a more polar zeolite structure. Zeolite Y has a completely different topology with larger pores and cages instead of channels.
texts.text: Materials. 1. Zeolites. ZSM-5 and silicalite were obtained from PQ Corp. (coded as CBV-3020 and CBV-3002, respectively) and zeolite Y was obtained from Zeocat. Table 1 lists their main characteristics. Both PQ zeolites have the same structure, consisting of intersecting channels forming a threedimensional network. They differ in hydrophobic character since a higher degree of isomorphous substitution (Al for Si) in the zeolite framework of CBV-3020 results in a more polar zeolite structure. Zeolite Y has a completely different topology with larger pores and cages instead of channels.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Polymer. The PDMS polymer is formed by two components (RTV 615A and B), both supplied by General Electric. Component A is the prepolymer and consists of a long PDMS chain terminated by a vinyl group, while component B, the cross-linker, contains several Si -H groups per polymer chain.
texts.text: Polymer. The PDMS polymer is formed by two components (RTV 615A and B), both supplied by General Electric. Component A is the prepolymer and consists of a long PDMS chain terminated by a vinyl group, while component B, the cross-linker, contains several Si -H groups per polymer chain.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Methods. 1. Membrane Preparation. Zeolite samples were equilibrated at a constant humidity of 80% for at least 24 h. They were weighed, and the exact dry weight was calculated by subtracting the water content which was determined gravimetrically. Zeolite Y and ZSM-5 were dispersed in methyl isobutyl ketone (MIBK), whereas chloroform was chosen as the solvent for silicalite. The zeolite dispersion was treated for 1 h in an ultrasonic bath in order to break the crystal aggregates. The cross-linker was then added to the zeolite/solvent suspension and the mixture was stirred for 2 h, after which the prepolymer was added. After stirring for another hour, the suspension was submitted to a vacuum treatment to remove air bubbles. Alayer of about 500 m of this suspension was then cast on a glass plate and cured for 45 min in a vacuum oven at 150 C. After

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Introduction
texts.text: Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The removal of chlorinated hydrocarbons (Cl-HC's) from wastewater is a major problem in several industries these days. All traditional methods used show important drawbacks, and pervaporation appears to be a useful alternative. The potentials of this technique for removing Cl-HC's have already been studied by several authors using self-prepared hydrophobic membranes 1,2 or commercially available PDMS(poly(dimethylsiloxane)) membranes. 3 -6 The highest selectivities were realized with silicalite-filled PDMS. 3
texts.text: The removal of chlorinated hydrocarbons (Cl-HC's) from wastewater is a major problem in several industries these days. All traditional methods used show important drawbacks, and pervaporation appears to be a useful alternative. The potentials of this technique for removing Cl-HC's have already been studied by several authors using self-prepared hydrophobic membranes 1,2 or commercially available PDMS(poly(dimethylsiloxane)) membranes. 3 -6 The highest selectivities were realized with silicalite-filled PDMS. 3

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Within one homologous series of alcohols, the pervaporation with PDMS membranes was found to be governed by sorption. 7 When molecules with different functional groups but the same number of carbon atoms were considered, diffusion was found to be the determining factor. 7 For Cl-HC's, however, the controlling factor seemed to vary, depending on the number of chlorine atoms present on the molecule. 1,2,8 Using zeolite-filled membranes, 3 -5,9 -13 even more complicated dependencies of performance on sorption or diffusion were found, as different sorption and diffusion in the two distinct phases had to be considered. In some cases, the zeolite -polymer interphase had to be considered to be (as) still another third phase. 14 It is the aim of this paper to gain more insight into the factors controlling pervaporation of Cl-HC's using zeolite-filled PDMS membranes. Sorption, diffusion, and pervaporation will be studied in detail.
texts.text: Within one homologous series of alcohols, the pervaporation with PDMS membranes was found to be governed by sorption. 7 When molecules with different functional groups but the same number of carbon atoms were considered, diffusion was found to be the determining factor. 7 For Cl-HC's, however, the controlling factor seemed to vary, depending on the number of chlorine atoms present on the molecule. 1,2,8 Using zeolite-filled membranes, 3 -5,9 -13 even more complicated dependencies of performance on sorption or diffusion were found, as different sorption and diffusion in the two distinct phases had to be considered. In some cases, the zeolite -polymer interphase had to be considered to be (as) still another third phase. 14 It is the aim of this paper to gain more insight into the factors controlling pervaporation of Cl-HC's using zeolite-filled PDMS membranes. Sorption, diffusion, and pervaporation will be studied in detail.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In a recent study by Dotremont et al., 6 the sorption and diffusion of Cl-HC's in commercially available membranes (GFT Co.) was investigated. The results will be used here as a reference to which the performance of PDMS membranes with different kinds of zeolite fillers (zeolite Y, ZSM-5, and silicalite) will be compared in the pervaporation of Cl-HC's from aqueous solutions. In part 1 of this series, the influence of the filler on sorption will be studied in detail. Five Cl-HC's were selected to study the equilibrium sorptions from the liquid phase and to record sorption isotherms from the vapor phase. The measurements were done on the fillers and on the unfilled PDMS as well as on the filled membranes. It was attempted to correlate the sorption results with molecular properties such as kinetic diameter and dipole moment (part 1). On the other hand, the

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2. Sorption Measurements. From the Liquid Phase. Sorption of pure liquids in the membranes was investigated on 2 2-cm membrane strips at room temperature. In order to desorb the water from the polymer and more particularly from the zeolite, the membrane was pretreated at 150 C under vacuum. The membranes were then immersed in the pure liquids during 48 h. The amount sorbed was determined by weight. The membrane surface was wiped dry, before weighing as quickly as possible, so as to minimize evaporation.
texts.text: 2. Sorption Measurements. From the Liquid Phase. Sorption of pure liquids in the membranes was investigated on 2 2-cm membrane strips at room temperature. In order to desorb the water from the polymer and more particularly from the zeolite, the membrane was pretreated at 150 C under vacuum. The membranes were then immersed in the pure liquids during 48 h. The amount sorbed was determined by weight. The membrane surface was wiped dry, before weighing as quickly as possible, so as to minimize evaporation.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Results and Discussion
texts.text: Results and Discussion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 1. Equilibrium Sorptions. Sorption in PDMS. Sorption from the liquid phase in pure PDMS was determined for five halogenated compounds (Table 2). Two series of compounds were considered. In the first series, hydrogen atoms are replaced by chlorine atoms on one carbon atom (C1 series). For the compounds having two carbon atoms (C2 series), the major difference is the occurrence of a double bond in C2HCl3. Figure 1 gives the liquid sorption capacity in pure PDMS as a function of the dipole moment and the kinetic diameter of the corresponding Cl-HC's. The sorption increases with decreasing dipole moment and remains at a maximum plateau value below a certain critical polar character of the sorbing substance. This plateau value corresponds to a maximum degree of swelling of the polymer, determined by its degree of cross-linking. Data on the kinetic diameter of the compounds clearly do not correlate with the sorption. In conclusion, polarity is the molecule property that solely determines sorption in PDMS for this series of compounds.
texts.text: 1. Equilibrium Sorptions. Sorption in PDMS. Sorption from the liquid phase in pure PDMS was determined for five halogenated compounds (Table 2). Two series of compounds were considered. In the first series, hydrogen atoms are replaced by chlorine atoms on one carbon atom (C1 series). For the compounds having two carbon atoms (C2 series), the major difference is the occurrence of a double bond in C2HCl3. Figure 1 gives the liquid sorption capacity in pure PDMS as a function of the dipole moment and the kinetic diameter of the corresponding Cl-HC's. The sorption increases with decreasing dipole moment and remains at a maximum plateau value below a certain critical polar character of the sorbing substance. This plateau value corresponds to a maximum degree of swelling of the polymer, determined by its degree of cross-linking. Data on the kinetic diameter of the compounds clearly do not correlate with the sorption. In conclusion, polarity is the molecule property that solely determines sorption in PDMS for this series of compounds.

texts.orig: was determined from the vapor phase. Unfortunately, it was impossible to perform both experiments in the same conditions. Indeed, sorption in a zeolite from a liquid phase is impossible to be quantified without using a solvent to dissolve the sorbate. Unfortunately, no nonsorbing solvents (necessary to avoid competitive adsorption) exist for zeolite Y. On the other hand, vapor-phase sorption in PDMS shows a high experimental error at a relative pressure of p / p 0 ) 1 due to too strong an asymptotic character of the isotherm at this relative pressure.
texts.text: was determined from the vapor phase. Unfortunately, it was impossible to perform both experiments in the same conditions. Indeed, sorption in a zeolite from a liquid phase is impossible to be quantified without using a solvent to dissolve the sorbate. Unfortunately, no nonsorbing solvents (necessary to avoid competitive adsorption) exist for zeolite Y. On the other hand, vapor-phase sorption in PDMS shows a high experimental error at a relative pressure of p / p 0 ) 1 due to too strong an asymptotic character of the isotherm at this relative pressure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 2 gives the sorption in the different types of zeolites for the molecules given in Table 2. Compared with PDMS sorption, completely different factors determine the sorption in the zeolites. Indeed, the zeolite framework is a rigid structure that cannot swell. When adding a chlorine atom in the C1 series, the volume occupied by one molecule increases. Since the maximum available sorption volume in the zeolite is limited, the sorption capacity of the compounds is lowered as the compound's volume increases. The enhanced sorption capacity for C2HCl3 compared with 1,1,2-C2H3Cl3 is due to its planar structure, allowing for a better packing of the molecules in the narrow zeolite channels or cages. When making a comparison between the three zeolites for each component, the same tendencies are observed. Due to the presence of large pores and large cages in zeolite Y, the capacity of this zeolite is highest. Hardly any difference can be noticed between ZSM-5 and silicalite, two zeolites with the same structure. For the zeolite, no relation between the sorption capacity and dipole moment could be found, opposite to the above-mentioned sorption in the polymer. On the other hand, a correlation was found here between the sorption capacity of the zeolites and the kinetic diameter of the sorbates, as shown in Figure 3: sorption decreases with increasing kinetic diameter.
texts.text: Figure 2 gives the sorption in the different types of zeolites for the molecules given in Table 2. Compared with PDMS sorption, completely different factors determine the sorption in the zeolites. Indeed, the zeolite framework is a rigid structure that cannot swell. When adding a chlorine atom in the C1 series, the volume occupied by one molecule increases. Since the maximum available sorption volume in the zeolite is limited, the sorption capacity of the compounds is lowered as the compound's volume increases. The enhanced sorption capacity for C2HCl3 compared with 1,1,2-C2H3Cl3 is due to its planar structure, allowing for a better packing of the molecules in the narrow zeolite channels or cages. When making a comparison between the three zeolites for each component, the same tendencies are observed. Due to the presence of large pores and large cages in zeolite Y, the capacity of this zeolite is highest. Hardly any difference can be noticed between ZSM-5 and silicalite, two zeolites with the same structure. For the zeolite, no relation between the sorption capacity and dipole moment could be found, opposite to the above-mentioned sorption in the polymer. On the other hand, a correlation was found here between the sorption capacity of the zeolites and the kinetic diameter of the sorbates, as shown in Figure 3: sorption decreases with increasing kinetic diameter.

texts.orig: PDMS, membranes were prepared with a filling degree as high as possible but without risking the introduction of pin holes. Filling degrees of 30 vol % for ZSM-5 and 40 vol % for silicalite and zeolite Y were chosen.
texts.text: PDMS, membranes were prepared with a filling degree as high as possible but without risking the introduction of pin holes. Filling degrees of 30 vol % for ZSM-5 and 40 vol % for silicalite and zeolite Y were chosen.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The amount of water sorbed was determined after immersing the membranes in pure water for 48 h. In comparison with the pure PDMS membranes (water sorption of 0.036 mL/g), a slight increase in sorption capacity of the silicalite (0.070 mL/g) and ZSM-5 (0.049 mL/g) filled membranes can be observed, indicating that the polarity of the membranes increases to a small extent upon incorporation of these fillers. The unexpected, slightly higher sorption capacity for the silicalite-filled membrane compared with the ZSM-5-filled one is probably attributed to the different zeolite content. The zeolite Y membrane shows the largest water sorption capacity (0.266 mL/g), confirming that the incorporation of the hydrophilic zeolite Y occurs with an important polarity increase of the membrane. 15
texts.text: The amount of water sorbed was determined after immersing the membranes in pure water for 48 h. In comparison with the pure PDMS membranes (water sorption of 0.036 mL/g), a slight increase in sorption capacity of the silicalite (0.070 mL/g) and ZSM-5 (0.049 mL/g) filled membranes can be observed, indicating that the polarity of the membranes increases to a small extent upon incorporation of these fillers. The unexpected, slightly higher sorption capacity for the silicalite-filled membrane compared with the ZSM-5-filled one is probably attributed to the different zeolite content. The zeolite Y membrane shows the largest water sorption capacity (0.266 mL/g), confirming that the incorporation of the hydrophilic zeolite Y occurs with an important polarity increase of the membrane. 15

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2. Sorption Isotherms. The sorption isotherms from the vapor phase for C2HCl3 and CCl4 were recorded in nonfilled PDMS, in silicalite, and in silicalite-filled PDMS membranes with different loadings (Figures 4 and 5). As expected, 16 two completely different types of sorption isotherms can be observed: a Langmuir-type sorption isotherm for the zeolite and a Flory -Huggins type isotherm for the polymer. The composite membranes display a combined type isotherm, showing more similarities with the Langmuir type as the zeolite content increases. At low activities, Langmuir sorption is predominant in the composite membranes: the sorption in the zeolite is much higher there than in the polymer phase. This is best observed in the C2HCl3 sorption, since sorption in the zeolite phase is highest for this compound. At high activities, the typical polymer sorption can be recognized, especially in the case of CCl4. This is different from the results of Bartels-Caspers et al., 17 who found that the sorption isotherms for the composite membranes and for the zeolite had similar shapes. Two reasons might explain the difference: firstly, the alcohols considered in Bartels-Caspers et al. show a much lower sorption in the polymer than in the zeolite. Secondly, we considered lower zeolite contents than these authors.
texts.text: 2. Sorption Isotherms. The sorption isotherms from the vapor phase for C2HCl3 and CCl4 were recorded in nonfilled PDMS, in silicalite, and in silicalite-filled PDMS membranes with different loadings (Figures 4 and 5). As expected, 16 two completely different types of sorption isotherms can be observed: a Langmuir-type sorption isotherm for the zeolite and a Flory -Huggins type isotherm for the polymer. The composite membranes display a combined type isotherm, showing more similarities with the Langmuir type as the zeolite content increases. At low activities, Langmuir sorption is predominant in the composite membranes: the sorption in the zeolite is much higher there than in the polymer phase. This is best observed in the C2HCl3 sorption, since sorption in the zeolite phase is highest for this compound. At high activities, the typical polymer sorption can be recognized, especially in the case of CCl4. This is different from the results of Bartels-Caspers et al., 17 who found that the sorption isotherms for the composite membranes and for the zeolite had similar shapes. Two reasons might explain the difference: firstly, the alcohols considered in Bartels-Caspers et al. show a much lower sorption in the polymer than in the zeolite. Secondly, we considered lower zeolite contents than these authors.

texts.orig: One of the aims of incorporating zeolites in dense membranes is to enhance the sorption capacity of the composite membrane under pervaporation conditions. Looking at the isotherms in Figures 4 and 5, predictions can be made in which activity

texts.orig: Figure 5. Sorption isotherms of tetrachloromethane in PDMS, silicalite, and filled PDMS.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: region pervaporation will benefit from a composite membrane, at least from the point of view of sorption. It was shown above that CCl4 sorbs less in the zeolite than C2HCl3 (Figure 2). For CCl4, the sorption capacity of the silicalite-filled membranes at low activity is only slightly improved by the zeolite incorporation. Moreover, from an activity of 0.5 and higher, the effect of the zeolite on sorption becomes negative since the sorption isotherm of pure PDMS crosses the one of pure silicalite. For C2HCl3, on the other hand, the positive effect of silicalite is much more pronounced: the sorption capacity of silicalite for C2HCl3 is much larger than for CCl4 (Figure 2). Consequently, the gain in sorption capacity at low activity values is much stronger. Moreover, the point of intersection between the curves of pure silicalite and PDMS is situated at much higher activities ( > 0.8). In conclusion, for the concerned sorption, a positive effect in pervaporation can be expected especially at low activities and in a much more pronounced way for compounds with high sorption in the zeolite.
texts.text: region pervaporation will benefit from a composite membrane, at least from the point of view of sorption. It was shown above that CCl4 sorbs less in the zeolite than C2HCl3 (Figure 2). For CCl4, the sorption capacity of the silicalite-filled membranes at low activity is only slightly improved by the zeolite incorporation. Moreover, from an activity of 0.5 and higher, the effect of the zeolite on sorption becomes negative since the sorption isotherm of pure PDMS crosses the one of pure silicalite. For C2HCl3, on the other hand, the positive effect of silicalite is much more pronounced: the sorption capacity of silicalite for C2HCl3 is much larger than for CCl4 (Figure 2). Consequently, the gain in sorption capacity at low activity values is much stronger. Moreover, the point of intersection between the curves of pure silicalite and PDMS is situated at much higher activities ( > 0.8). In conclusion, for the concerned sorption, a positive effect in pervaporation can be expected especially at low activities and in a much more pronounced way for compounds with high sorption in the zeolite.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Earlier, it was shown that the zeolite and the polymer do not appear as two noninteracting phases. 17 -22 A physical or maybe even a chemical cross-linking effect of the zeolite was shown to be exerted on the polymer. To confirm this statement, a theoretical sorption capacity was calculated by adding up the sorption isotherms of the individual components, i.e., silicalite and PDMS, taking into account their partition in the membrane. As shown in Figure 6a, the theoretical sorption capacity is larger than the experimentally measured one for C2HCl3. The discrepancy is larger the more zeolite is incorporated. It indicates indeed that structural changes are introduced in the polymer by the incorporation of silicalite, making the polymer system denser. The same observation was also made for the theoretical and experimental isotherms of CCl4 (Figure 6b).
texts.text: Earlier, it was shown that the zeolite and the polymer do not appear as two noninteracting phases. 17 -22 A physical or maybe even a chemical cross-linking effect of the zeolite was shown to be exerted on the polymer. To confirm this statement, a theoretical sorption capacity was calculated by adding up the sorption isotherms of the individual components, i.e., silicalite and PDMS, taking into account their partition in the membrane. As shown in Figure 6a, the theoretical sorption capacity is larger than the experimentally measured one for C2HCl3. The discrepancy is larger the more zeolite is incorporated. It indicates indeed that structural changes are introduced in the polymer by the incorporation of silicalite, making the polymer system denser. The same observation was also made for the theoretical and experimental isotherms of CCl4 (Figure 6b).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 3. Comparison with Commercial Membranes. In the next paragraphs, membranes and zeolites will be used that were obtained from GFT -Le Carbone (Neunkirchen-Heinitz, Germany). They will be referred to as 'GFT silicalite membranes (GFT-sil-PDMS)' (40 wt % PDMS/60 wt % silicalite ) 46 vol %), 'GFT PDMS membranes (GFT-PDMS)' (pure PDMS), or 'GFT silicalite (GFT-sil)'. The membranes used in the previous paragraphs will be referred to as 'synthesized membranes'.
texts.text: 3. Comparison with Commercial Membranes. In the next paragraphs, membranes and zeolites will be used that were obtained from GFT -Le Carbone (Neunkirchen-Heinitz, Germany). They will be referred to as 'GFT silicalite membranes (GFT-sil-PDMS)' (40 wt % PDMS/60 wt % silicalite ) 46 vol %), 'GFT PDMS membranes (GFT-PDMS)' (pure PDMS), or 'GFT silicalite (GFT-sil)'. The membranes used in the previous paragraphs will be referred to as 'synthesized membranes'.

texts.orig: These sorption experiments were extended to GFT-sil-PDMS membranes, unfilled GFT PDMS membranes (toplayers only), and the synthesized membranes as shown in Figure 8. For the unfilled membranes, the amount of organics sorbed in GFTPDMS is much larger than in the synthesized membrane. Furthermore, the plateau value that was observed in Figure 1 for the synthesized PDMS is less explicit now. Both observations indicate that the GFT PDMS membranes can swell to a larger extent than the synthesized ones, probably due to a lower degree of cross-linking.
texts.text: These sorption experiments were extended to GFT-sil-PDMS membranes, unfilled GFT PDMS membranes (toplayers only), and the synthesized membranes as shown in Figure 8. For the unfilled membranes, the amount of organics sorbed in GFTPDMS is much larger than in the synthesized membrane. Furthermore, the plateau value that was observed in Figure 1 for the synthesized PDMS is less explicit now. Both observations indicate that the GFT PDMS membranes can swell to a larger extent than the synthesized ones, probably due to a lower degree of cross-linking.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There is a remarkable difference in water sorption capacity between both membranes: the amount sorbed in the unfilled GFT-PDMS is much larger than in the synthesized PDMS. The difference is too large to be accounted for by merely a difference in cross-linking. The GFT-PDMS is therefore assumed to be more polar than the PDMS delivered by General Electric. This was expected since the GFT-PDMS contains rather polar acrylate groups, which are absent in the General Electric PDMS.

This was expected since the GFT-PDMS contains rather polar acrylate groups, which are absent in the General Electric PDMS.

On the other hand, when looking at the synthesized membranes, the addition of the zeolite makes the composite membrane more polar. Indeed, for the self-prepared membranes,

texts.orig: Maximum Sorption Capacities. For C2HCl3, the same types of sorption isotherms from the vapor phase are found for the filled GFT-membrane as for the synthesized filled membrane (Figure 9). However, there is a difference in sorption capacity: a somewhat enhanced sorption capacity is observed for the GFT materials, both the zeolite and the polymer. For the PDMS phase, this was ascribed already to a less dense cross-linking. The higher sorption for the GFT filler may have two explanations. Firstly, a more pure zeolite phase may be used containing fewer amorphous regions or better calcined pore structures. Secondly, a more polar character of the zeolite may show a better affinity toward the rather polar C2HCl3. The latter explanation is most probable in view of the results presented in Figure 7. Further confirmation is found in the vapor sorption experiments with CCl4 (Figure 10). The figure clearly shows that the incorporation of silicalite has a much larger negative
texts.text: Maximum Sorption Capacities. For C2HCl3, the same types of sorption isotherms from the vapor phase are found for the filled GFT-membrane as for the synthesized filled membrane (Figure 9). However, there is a difference in sorption capacity: a somewhat enhanced sorption capacity is observed for the GFT materials, both the zeolite and the polymer. For the PDMS phase, this was ascribed already to a less dense cross-linking. The higher sorption for the GFT filler may have two explanations. Firstly, a more pure zeolite phase may be used containing fewer amorphous regions or better calcined pore structures. Secondly, a more polar character of the zeolite may show a better affinity toward the rather polar C2HCl3. The latter explanation is most probable in view of the results presented in Figure 7. Further confirmation is found in the vapor sorption experiments with CCl4 (Figure 10). The figure clearly shows that the incorporation of silicalite has a much larger negative

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The cross-linking action of silicalite on PDMS, already mentioned above and in other reports, 17 -22 was also studied for the GFT membranes. In Figure 11, the experimental curve for CCl4 sorption in the GFT silicalite membrane is compared with the theoretically calculated sorption curve. Both curves coincide, or sometimes the experimental values are even higher than those calculated. This is in complete contrast with Figure 6b and indicates that the acrylate groups present in the GFT PDMS don't allow cross-linking with the zeolite. Figure 11 suggests that both phases in the GFT membranes are noninteracting or maybe even that a bad adhesion might occur as the experimental values tend to lie slightly above the theoretical values.
texts.text: The cross-linking action of silicalite on PDMS, already mentioned above and in other reports, 17 -22 was also studied for the GFT membranes. In Figure 11, the experimental curve for CCl4 sorption in the GFT silicalite membrane is compared with the theoretically calculated sorption curve. Both curves coincide, or sometimes the experimental values are even higher than those calculated. This is in complete contrast with Figure 6b and indicates that the acrylate groups present in the GFT PDMS don't allow cross-linking with the zeolite. Figure 11 suggests that both phases in the GFT membranes are noninteracting or maybe even that a bad adhesion might occur as the experimental values tend to lie slightly above the theoretical values.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Conclusions
texts.text: Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to improve the performance of membranes in the pervaporation of chlorinated hydrocarbons, sorption effects were studied in zeolite-filled PDMS membranes. Whereas sorption in the zeolite is mainly determined by the size of the studied molecules, sorption in the PDMS matrix follows polarity. Incorporation of zeolite Y decreased the hydrophobic character of the PDMS, while isotherms indicated a positive influence of silicalite incorporation on the membrane sorption. This positive effect was limited to activities below a certain critical value. The lower the sorption of the compound in the zeolite phase or the higher the sorption in the polymer phase, the lower this limiting activity was.
texts.text: In order to improve the performance of membranes in the pervaporation of chlorinated hydrocarbons, sorption effects were studied in zeolite-filled PDMS membranes. Whereas sorption in the zeolite is mainly determined by the size of the studied molecules, sorption in the PDMS matrix follows polarity. Incorporation of zeolite Y decreased the hydrophobic character of the PDMS, while isotherms indicated a positive influence of silicalite incorporation on the membrane sorption. This positive effect was limited to activities below a certain critical value. The lower the sorption of the compound in the zeolite phase or the higher the sorption in the polymer phase, the lower this limiting activity was.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the comparison between commercially available membranes and the synthesized ones, sorption experiments showed a more polar structure for both the commercial polymer and the zeolite. When comparing the zeolite and the polymer within one set, the zeolite was more polar with respect to the polymer for the synthesized membrane, while the opposite was observed in the commercial products. Across-linking action of the zeolite on the polymer was proven for the self prepared membranes but was absent in the GFT membranes.
texts.text: In the comparison between commercially available membranes and the synthesized ones, sorption experiments showed a more polar structure for both the commercial polymer and the zeolite. When comparing the zeolite and the polymer within one set, the zeolite was more polar with respect to the polymer for the synthesized membrane, while the opposite was observed in the commercial products. Across-linking action of the zeolite on the polymer was proven for the self prepared membranes but was absent in the GFT membranes.
